title: Absence of a Superradiant Phase Transition in Dirac Landau Polaritons
authors: Elsa Jöchl, Felix Helmrich, Frieder Lindel, Lucy Hale, Lorenzo Graziotto, Mona Jarrahi, Tobia F. Nova, Jérôme Faist, Giacomo Scalari
abstract: The condensation of photons into a macroscopically populated ground state, a superradiant phase transition (SRPT), is one of the most striking predictions of cavity quantum electrodynamics (cQED), yet has resisted experimental realization in equilibrium for over fifty years. Whether such a transition can survive at all in light-matter coupled systems is still debated, with a widely established \textit{No-Go} theorem ruling it out across several models. Graphene cyclotron transitions ultrastrongly coupled to terahertz (THz) cavities have been at the heart of this debate. At leading order, the linear Dirac dispersion of electrons does not generate the diamagnetic $\vec{A}^2$ term that enforces the theorem, making graphene the cleanest candidate for a No-Go-evading phase transition.   Here, we present the first THz spectroscopic measurements of an encapsulated monolayer graphene flake ultrastrongly coupled to a single complementary split-ring resonator. By tuning the carrier density we sweep the system from weak coupling into the ultrastrong regime, reaching a normalized coupling $Ω_\text{R}/ω_\text{cav} \approx 0.4$. This is well into the range where a second order SRPT would manifest by a softening of the lower polariton branch, which we do not observe. The full polariton dispersion is instead quantitatively reproduced by a Hopfield Hamiltonian derived from first principles using a near-field model that accounts for the sub-wavelength character of the cavity.   Our results establish an experimental baseline for predictions of cavity-driven phase transitions in two-dimensional Dirac systems, and rule out a No-Go-evading SRPT in graphene Landau polaritons up to the strongest couplings accessible today, with direct implications for proposals invoking vacuum-induced order in solid-state cQED.
summary: This paper experimentally investigates the possibility of a superradiant phase transition in graphene-based Landau polaritons. By measuring the polariton dispersion in the ultrastrong coupling regime, the authors demonstrate that the system follows the Hopfield model and lacks the mode softening predicted by the Dicke model. This result effectively rules out a No-Go-evading phase transition in these graphene systems at current experimental scales.
main_problem: Investigating whether a superradiant phase transition (SRPT) can occur in graphene Landau polaritons, specifically testing if the system evades the 'No-Go' theorem that suppresses such transitions due to the diamagnetic A^2 term.
main_result: The experiment rules out a No-Go-evading SRPT in graphene Landau polaritons at accessible coupling strengths, finding that the system follows the Hopfield model rather than the Dicke model.
method: THz time-domain spectroscopy was used on an encapsulated monolayer graphene flake coupled to a split-ring resonator, with carrier density tuned via electrostatic gating and magnetic fields applied to induce Landau levels.
model_or_system: The system consists of an encapsulated monolayer graphene flake ultrastrongly coupled to a single complementary split-ring resonator (cSRR). The physics is analyzed by comparing the Dicke model (which predicts SRPT) against the Hopfield Hamiltonian (which includes the diamagnetic A^2 term).
key_observables: Upper and lower polariton branches, polariton splitting, cyclotron frequencies, and THz transmission spectra.
important_parameters: Normalized coupling strength (up to Omega_R/omega_cav approx 0.4), carrier density (n), magnetic field (B), and cyclotron mass (m*).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the near-field model for the sub-wavelength cavity and is limited by the experimental ability to reach the predicted critical density for the phase transition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares Dicke vs. Hopfield model predictions; Figure 2 shows spectral maps of polariton evolution; Figure 4 shows the UP frequency blue-shift (confirming Hopfield) and the lack of LP mode softening (ruling out SRPT).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SRPT debate and the No-Go theorem, describes the graphene-cSRR experimental platform, presents THz spectroscopic results showing polariton dispersion, compares these results to Dicke and Hopfield models, and concludes with the implications for vacuum-induced order.
why_interesting: It addresses a fundamental debate in cavity QED regarding the existence of equilibrium phase transitions in light-matter systems and provides experimental evidence for the role of the diamagnetic term in the ultrastrong coupling regime.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Probing SRPTs in monolayer graphene. a) Dispersion of the lower and upper polariton branches as a function of density n (coupling η) at resonance in the Dicke (grey dashed lines) and Hopfield models (purple lines). While the Dicke prediction exhibits criticality with softening to zero frequency of the lower branch, the Hopfield model does not. The density range where a superradiant phase (SRP) is predicted in the Dicke model is indicated by the grey background. b) Top view of the sample before (left) and after (right) the deposition of the cSRR. c) Schematic view of the experimental setup. The sample consisting of the graphene flake and the cSRR is mounted between silicon immersion...

FIG. 2. Tuning of cyclotron dispersion and light-matter coupling in monolayer graphene. THz transmission spectroscopy as a function of the magnetic field with monolayer graphene biased at a) the CNP, b) an intermediate density n = 1.97 1012/cm2, and c) n = 7.05 1012/cm2, performed at a temperature of 3 K. The fitted cavity and cyclotron frequencies are indicated in each spectral map as white dashed and dashed-dotted lines, respectively. All data are referenced to the transmission spectrum of the bare SiO2/Si substrate. The contrast of the color map is normalized to its maximum in each map separately. The inset of panel c) highlights a splitting of the LP branch by enhancing the contrast by...

FIG. 3. Density dependence of ultrastrong light-matter coupling in graphene. a) Magnetic field-dependent spectral map at an electron density n = 7.05 1012/cm2 overlaid with the Hopfield model fit (black line) to the fitted peak positions of the respective spectra (white dots). FIG. 4. Absence of SRPT. a-c) Normalized peak frequencies extracted from Lorentzian fits for a) the UP asymptote at B = 0 T, b) the LP asymptote at B = 6 T, and c) the UP and LP at the anticrossing magnetic field B× for each respective density. Hopfield and Dicke model predictions are overlaid for each case in solid colored and dashed gray lines, respectively. The shaded area indicate the spectral resolution of the measurements. d) Measured THz transmittance spectra at B× (varying with density, as indicated on the right).
